author: Ignatius of Antioch
note: Ignatius calls Jesus 'our God' c. AD 107 — 218 years before Nicaea. Verbatim-confirmed against the Clementson source (apologia-citations, 2026-07-12). Preferred citation translation for publication: Roberts-Donaldson (ANF), to be swapped when a clean ANF source is reachable.
text: Ignatius, also called Theophorus, to the most blessed church, which is in Ephesus of Asia, bids much joy in Jesus Christ and in his spotless grace.

author: Ignatius of Antioch
note: The 'one Physician' passage — full deity and full humanity of Christ in one breath, pre-Nicene. Verbatim-confirmed against the Clementson source; the trailing ellipsis marks the omitted continuation ('first passible and then impassible, Jesus Christ our Lord'). Roberts-Donaldson renders the key phrase 'God existing in flesh'.
text: There is one physician, both having a body and being a spirit, born and not born, made flesh, yet God; in death, yet true life; both of Mary and of God …

author: Ignatius of Antioch
note: Verbatim-confirmed against the Clementson source; trailing ellipsis marks the omitted continuation.
text: Our God Jesus the anointed was carried in the womb by Mary according to the dispensation of God …

author: Ignatius of Antioch
note: Against docetism — the reality of Christ's flesh, crucifixion, and bodily resurrection. CORRECTED 2026-07-12: the earlier text was a composite that spliced in Roberts-Donaldson wording and altered spelling; this is clean single-translation Clementson text, ellipses marking the omitted (OCR-damaged) spans.
text: he is truly of the race of David according to the flesh, the Son of God, according to the will and power of God, having been truly made of a virgin, baptized by John … truly nailed for us in his body under Pontius Pilate and Herod the Tetrarch … And he suffered truly, as he also truly raised up himself: not, as some unbelievers say, that he suffered in appearance.

author: Athanasius of Alexandria
work: On the Incarnation of the Word (De Incarnatione)
note: The most-quoted line of the treatise. Wording and §54 citation corroborated via search (incl. a CCEL snippet), but NOT yet confirmed against the primary NPNF text (CCEL is network-blocked in the build sandbox) — flip to verified when a clean fetch is possible.
text: For He was made man that we might be made God; and He manifested Himself by a body that we might receive the idea of the unseen Father; and He endured the insolence of men that we might inherit immortality.
